title: SpaceX Buys Cursor, Betting $60B on Capture as AI Gets Cheaper - TCR 06/17/26
description:
SpaceX touched a $2.97 trillion valuation and agreed to buy AI coding app Cursor for $60 billion in stock, leaked audited filings showed OpenAI's $13.07 billion in 2025 revenue dwarfed by $19.18 billion in R&D alone, and France's intelligence service dropped Palantir for the homegrown firm ChapsVision to escape "strategic dependency."

https://sharedsapience.com/the-century-report-june-17-2026/

0:00 SpaceX's $2.97T day and OpenAI's losses
2:32 The cyber-capability fight outruns the blockade
5:17 France drops Palantir for ChapsVision
7:35 Northeast states plan a shared offshore wind grid
10:15 AI moves into the eldercare home
11:38 Synthesis and Closing

This episode covers the SpaceX-Cursor $60 billion deal and OpenAI's leaked losses, the export-control standoff outrunning the blockade as a max-critical Microsoft Copilot flaw surfaces, France swapping Palantir for ChapsVision, nine Northeast states publishing a shared offshore wind grid plan as the federal wind fight ends, and an always-on AI microphone moving into eldercare homes.

Two ledgers of the AI buildout arrived together. SpaceX overtook Amazon to become the world's fifth most valuable company and spent $60 billion in stock on Cursor, an app with a million developers and no path to breaking even, while leaked filings showed OpenAI spending far more on research than it earns, losses widening past $20 billion as inference costs scale with every prompt. The signal underneath: the cost of producing intelligence keeps falling and keeps getting easier to reproduce. The same loss of leverage ran through the day, as a frontier-model export blockade closed without resolution while the flagged capability already runs in open weights, a new safety nonprofit launched to verify what the labs claim, and France dropped Palantir for a domestic provider to escape a dependency its home government could switch off. Beneath the friction, nine Northeast states published technical standards for a shared offshore wind grid the same day the federal wind blockade collapsed in court, AI began naming the hardest plant specimens, in some cases faster than human specialists, and Genesis AI unveiled a general-purpose robot built around the work instead of the human shape.
